summary: High‑energy Honolulu set with epic Easy Wind and New Speedway jams
blurb: The show rides a hot Hawaiian vibe, highlighted by a soaring Easy Wind and extended New Speedway Boogie/Good Lovin' jams. Phil’s bass drives the early set while Bob steers the Good Lovin' improvisations. Recording quality is mixed, but the performance remains a notable snapshot of the 1970 Dead.
review: June 13, 1970 – Honolulu Civic Auditorium: The Dead turned the island into a summer playground, opening with a breezy Cold Rain And Snow that quickly gave way to an electrifying Easy Wind. Phil Lesh’s bass roars forward, often taking center stage in the mix, while Jerry Garcia fights his way through a muddy matrix that sometimes buries his lead tones. Bob Weir leads the band into a monster Good Lovin' jam, sandwiched around a crisp drum duet, before launching into a double‑dose of New Speedway Boogie where a surprise guest keyboard adds extra sparkle. The set closes with a rousing Turn On Your Lovelight*, leaving fans buzzing despite the tape cutting out just shy of the finale.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The single recording analysis labels the overall sentiment as mixed: strong praise for Easy Wind, New Speedway Boogie and Good Lovin' jams is balanced by comments about muddy early audience sections and a mix that buries Jerry's guitar. This yields a neutral adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Provides the most complete set despite mix quirks; captures the full performance while offering clearer board sections later in the show.

key_highlights: Epic Easy Wind that showcases Phil’s driving bass.
Extended New Speedway Boogie/Good Lovin' jams with a surprise guest keyboard.
High‑energy Hawaiian vibe that keeps the band on fire throughout the set.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar often gets buried in the matrix mix, making his leads harder to hear despite strong playing.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh drives the early set with a prominent, sometimes overly loud bass that propels Easy Wind and other jams.
band_performance.Bob: Weir takes charge of the Good Lovin' improvisations, delivering energetic rhythm work and lead fills.
band_performance.Mickey: Both drummers deliver razor‑sharp rhythms, though the snare buzz and occasional background tap are noticeable in the mix.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming locks in with Mickey, adding power to the jams despite some mix obscurity.
